notes.anchor: I want the commonwealth at last to be put on a firm footing
notes.note: Written in mid-April 44 BC, a month after Caesar's assassination, this phrase encodes Cicero's hope that the conspirators' act will restore republican government; by 'commonwealth on a firm footing' he means a restored Senate-led res publica, not a new autocracy.

notes.anchor: the promise you make in your letter
notes.note: Bithynicus had promised (in the letter preserved as Fam. 6.16) to come to Rome and live near Cicero if the political situation improved; Cicero treats this as a binding promissum and lists it as one of his personal reasons to want republican restoration.

notes.anchor: the regard your father, a most distinguished man, had for me
notes.note: Cicero alludes to the elder Bithynicus's friendship with him — probably a Pompeian connection — while noting that his own bond with the younger man surpasses any mere inherited obligation; the compliment ('most distinguished man') is a standard Roman obituary formula for a dead patron.

notes.anchor: those who have prospered at the times that have favoured them are more closely bound to you than I am; in family ties, no one is
notes.note: A careful self-positioning: Cicero admits that Caesar's beneficiaries owe Bithynicus more material gratitude than he does, but claims superiority in genuine affection — a way of distinguishing himself from the transactional patronage of the Caesarian faction now dominant in Rome.
